stories.headline: Broncos Ownership Group Acquires 40% Stake in Colorado Rockies in $672 Million Deal
stories.summary:
Greg and Carrie Walton Penner, who lead the Denver Broncos ownership group through Penner Sports Group, have purchased a 40% minority stake in the Colorado Rockies, becoming the franchise's largest minority owners. MLB formally approved the deal, which is valued at approximately $672 million based on Forbes' $1.68 billion team valuation. Dick Monfort announced the move Friday, and the Penners will have no day-to-day involvement in running the club.

The investment allows the Rockies to retire all outstanding debt and provides additional capital for the franchise, potentially enabling the team to become more competitive in baseball's uneven financial landscape. The Rockies' current 40-man roster payroll sits at $120.5 million, ranking 21st in MLB, far behind the league-leading New York Mets at $357.2 million. The Denver Post, a longtime minority owner, was the only existing shareholder to sell its entire stake as part of the transaction.

Dick Monfort will remain as chairman and CEO, Charlie Monfort in his ownership role, and Walker Monfort as club president handling day-to-day operations. Greg Penner will continue as CEO of the Broncos, and Carrie Penner will maintain her various roles with the football team. The Penners said the investment reflects their 'deep appreciation for what the Rockies mean to this region.'

stories.headline: Man Convicted of First-Degree Murder for Beating Denver Resident to Death After Car Break-In Confrontation
stories.summary:
A Denver jury on Friday found Stig Strong, 36, guilty of first-degree murder in the 2023 death of 34-year-old Jonathan Douglas, who was beaten to death on the sidewalk near his home in the La Alma-Lincoln Park neighborhood. Douglas was found unresponsive on the 1200 block of Mariposa Street on the morning of April 18, 2023. Investigators determined he had confronted Strong while Strong was breaking into cars in the area.

Surveillance video captured a man checking car door handles along Mariposa Street the night before Douglas was found, then approaching another person who fell to the ground. Police traced Douglas' cell phone and located Strong, who was found with Douglas' house and car keys in his possession at the time of his arrest. Douglas' home had also been burglarized.

The jury also convicted Strong of assault, aggravated robbery, and burglary. He faces a mandatory sentence of life in prison without the possibility of parole under Colorado law for the first-degree murder conviction. A sentencing hearing has not yet been scheduled.

stories.headline: Denver Man Sentenced to 56 Years for Strangling Jail Cellmate, on Top of Adams County Murder Sentences
stories.summary:
Ricky Roybal-Smith was sentenced Friday to 56 years in prison for the strangulation murder of his jail cellmate, Vincent Chacon, 34, at the Downtown Detention Center on June 30, 2025. Denver District Attorney John Walsh announced the sentence, which includes 48 years for second-degree murder and eight years for a prior driving under the influence conviction. The Denver sentence will run concurrently with Roybal-Smith's Adams County sentences.

In late March, Roybal-Smith pleaded guilty to two additional counts of second-degree murder for fatally stabbing two men — 27-year-old Jesse Shafer and 61-year-old Scott Davenport — on East Colfax Avenue in Aurora on June 29, 2025, the day before the jail killing. Adams County District Court Judge Brett Martin sentenced him to two consecutive 40-year terms for those stabbings. Roybal-Smith had been booked into the Downtown Detention Center on a hit-and-run charge the same night as the Aurora stabbings.

Roybal-Smith has a lengthy criminal history, including a 2016 conviction for vehicular assault-DUI that resulted in a 12-year prison sentence, and a 2022 arrest for threatening a family with a knife at an Englewood Walmart. DA Walsh said the combined sentences ensure Roybal-Smith 'will spend the rest of his life in prison.'

stories.headline: Series of Violent Incidents Strikes Metro Denver in 24-Hour Span, Leaving Two Dead
stories.summary:
The Denver metro area experienced a cluster of violent incidents between Thursday afternoon and early Friday morning, including two downtown Denver shootings, a fatal officer-involved shooting in Aurora, and a stabbing in Thornton. In the first Denver incident, Kaylen Jay Stroter, 22, was arrested after allegedly shooting a man walking his dog near Mestivo-Curtis Park at about 12:45 p.m. Thursday, killing him. A HALO street camera captured Stroter dropping a handgun as he walked away from the scene. He faces a charge of first-degree murder.

A second shooting occurred around 2:15 p.m. Thursday in the 2000 block of Lawrence Street, near Coors Field, sending a victim to the hospital with injuries that were not described as fatal. No suspect has been identified in that incident. In Thornton, a stabbing with non-life-threatening injuries was reported early Friday on the 1400 block of East 104th Avenue, and a suspect was arrested.

In Aurora, officers responding to a mental health crisis call in the 14000 block of East Stanford Circle encountered a 23-year-old man who allegedly charged at an officer with a knife, stabbing the officer multiple times in the head and also stabbing a police K9. Officers used less-than-lethal force with no effect before the stabbed officer shot the man, who was later pronounced dead at the hospital. The officer, a 20-year veteran, underwent surgery and is expected to survive.

stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston, Rep. Crow Travel to New Orleans to Pitch Denver as 2028 Democratic Convention Host
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston, U.S. Rep. Jason Crow, and Colorado Democratic Party Chair Shad Murib traveled to New Orleans on Friday to make Denver's case before Democratic National Committee delegates at the party's spring meeting. Denver is competing against Atlanta, Boston, Chicago, and Philadelphia to host the 2028 Democratic National Convention, scheduled for Aug. 7-10, 2028. The DNC is expected to make a final selection this summer following site visits to each finalist city.

Crow, who represents Aurora, argued Denver's successful hosting of the 2008 convention — when Barack Obama accepted the presidential nomination — demonstrates the city's capacity and infrastructure. Johnston's spokesman said landing the convention 'would be an enormous economic boon for Denver, bringing tens of thousands of visitors to Colorado and generating hundreds of millions of dollars in activity for the city and local businesses.' Three Denver City Council members are also part of the delegation in New Orleans.

Murib, who has been in New Orleans since Monday, said the delegation is emphasizing the seamless experience Denver can offer delegates from the airport to hotels to the convention venue. Colorado's track record as a reliably Democratic state in recent election cycles is also part of the pitch, with Crow describing Colorado as 'a beacon of how to do it right' for the national party.

stories.headline: Two Colorado Democratic Lawmakers Face Open-Records Lawsuit Over Vail Retreat Documents
stories.summary:
Denver progressive activist Derrick Blanton filed a lawsuit Friday in Denver District Court against state Sen. Lindsey Daugherty, Rep. Sean Camacho, and the Colorado Opportunity Caucus they co-chair, alleging violations of the Colorado Open Records Act. The suit demands records related to a Vail retreat organized by the caucus in October, including documents on costs, fundraising, and attendance. The caucus — a group of more than two dozen Democrats who are generally more moderate on business issues — has denied having responsive records.

Blanton's attorney, Scott Moss, argued in the suit that it is implausible the caucus generated no records for a multi-day retreat with overnight stays at a luxury mountain resort. Moss cited a grid he obtained through other means suggesting organizations were charged between $25,000 and $100,000 to attend the retreat and give presentations to the lawmakers present. The lawsuit also asks a judge to rule on whether the Opportunity Caucus is subject to CORA requests — a legal question that has not been tested in the courts.

Daugherty said in a statement that she and the caucus turned over records 'we were legally required to provide' and called the suit 'frivolous.' The case is the latest front in a broader intraparty clash: Colorado Common Cause previously filed ethics complaints against nearly every lawmaker who attended the retreat, alleging violations of a constitutional gift ban, and the state ethics commission opened an investigation. The caucus was formed in early 2025 with assistance from One Main Street, a dark money group that supported several caucus members' campaigns.

stories.headline: Federal Judge Extends Restraining Order on $6.2 Billion Merger of Fox31 and 9News Parent Companies
stories.summary:
A federal judge on Friday extended an emergency restraining order blocking a $6.2 billion merger between Nexstar Media Group and Tegna for one additional week, saying he needed more time to prepare a ruling on whether a longer preliminary injunction is warranted. Eight state attorneys general, including Colorado's Phil Weiser, and DirecTV filed suit to stop the deal, arguing it would raise consumer prices and harm local journalism. U.S. District Court Chief Judge Troy L. Nunley in Sacramento also modified the order to allow both companies to take routine business steps such as meeting federal debt reporting deadlines.

In Denver, Nexstar owns KDVR-Fox31 and KWGN-CW2, while Tegna owns NBC affiliate KUSA-9News and KTVD-My20. The proposed merger, approved by the FCC under the Trump administration through a waiver of ownership rules, would create a company controlling 265 television stations across 44 states and the District of Columbia. Nexstar has agreed to sell KTVD within two years as part of the FCC approval.

Nexstar's attorneys contend the deal will expand local journalism and programming. Opponents argue the combined company would gain enough leverage over pay-TV distributors to demand higher retransmission fees, with the judge noting that a refusal to pay could cost subscribers access to programming including Sunday NFL games. The extended restraining order remains in effect through April 17.

stories.headline: Colorado Bill Would Allow Towns to Be Declared Abandoned More Quickly, Spurred by Hartman Water Crisis
stories.summary:
A bipartisan pair of Colorado lawmakers introduced Senate Bill 157 this week to make it easier for counties and residents to seek an official abandonment declaration for towns that have lost their governing bodies and are operating failing critical infrastructure. The bill was sponsored by state Sens. Rod Pelton, a Cheyenne Wells Republican, and Nick Hinrichsen, a Pueblo Democrat, in direct response to the collapse of local government in Hartman, a Prowers County community of roughly 30 people near the Kansas border.

Hartman has operated without a Board of Trustees since January, when all members resigned following years of infighting and a violent altercation that sent one trustee to the hospital. With no clerk to hold elections and no trustees to hire a clerk, the town has been in governmental limbo. More urgently, Hartman's water system is on the verge of failure, with chlorine supplies running low and a six-figure fine pending from the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment.

Under current law, a town must wait five years before the secretary of state can declare it abandoned. The new bill would allow a county, landowner, or registered elector to apply immediately for abandonment when a town lacks both a board and a clerk and operates critical water infrastructure. It would also unlock $100,000 in state grant funding for water system maintenance during the abandonment process. The governor's office, attorney general's office, and Prowers County officials all participated in drafting the legislation.

stories.headline: Denver Metro Housing Market Rebounds in March with 31% Surge in Pending Sales
stories.summary:
The Denver metro housing market posted a significant rebound in March after a slow start to 2026, with pending sales rising 31 percent from February, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' monthly report. The median days on the MLS dropped by half to just 16 days, indicating heightened buyer urgency, and the median closing price rose approximately 3 percent to $590,000. The gains came despite mortgage rates remaining above 6 percent and financial market volatility.

DMAR Market Trends Committee Chair Amanda Snitker said the March activity reflects a market that has been 'quietly finding its balance for three years' as inventory has normalized and pricing has reset. Well-priced homes in desirable locations received multiple offers, and new listings for homes priced at $1 million or more rose 8 percent in the first quarter compared to the same period last year, though closed transactions in that segment were up only 1.4 percent.

Luxury properties are taking considerably longer to sell than in prior years: homes over $1 million averaged 62 days on the market through the first quarter of 2026, compared to a 24-day average in 2022. Agents are advising sellers to set realistic pricing expectations and invest in high-quality presentation to attract the segment's more discerning buyers. If March momentum holds, modest price increases and competitive conditions are expected to continue through spring.

stories.headline: Boulder County Sheriff Seeks Public Help to Identify Woman Critically Injured by Lightning Strike in Superior
stories.summary:
The Boulder County Sheriff's Office is asking the public to help identify a woman between 20 and 30 years old who was struck by lightning Thursday evening on a walking path behind the 1000 block of Lasalle Street in the Town of Superior. Bystanders heard a loud boom around 5:39 p.m., found the woman unresponsive beneath a tree, and began CPR before calling 911. She was airlifted to a Denver-area hospital and remains in critical condition, though she has a regular heart rate and is breathing on her own.

The woman was not carrying identification, and her cell phone was too badly damaged to retrieve information. The sheriff's office has attempted fingerprinting and checked missing persons reports in Boulder County without success. Officials described the woman as approximately 5'5", white, around 120 pounds with auburn hair, and three butterfly tattoos on her right upper arm. She was wearing a green running shirt, grey/black windbreaker, black running shorts with white stripes, and white and pink Saucony shoes.

The sheriff's office is asking residents in the Superior area to review home security and Ring camera footage from between 4:30 p.m. and 5:39 p.m. on April 9 that might show the woman running nearby, and to contact the non-emergency line at (303) 441-4444 with any information or footage.

stories.headline: Colorado First Responders Adopt Statewide Initiative to Standardize Wildfire Response Across Agencies
stories.summary:
More than 100 agencies across Colorado have participated in workshops tied to a new statewide initiative called Wildfire Initiated Community Conflagration, aimed at creating consistent best practices and shared language for responding to fast-moving wildfires. The program, which involves Douglas County, Boulder County, Larkspur Fire, and numerous other Front Range agencies, covers topics including risk profiling, alert and evacuation procedures, effective fire response, and large-scale incident command and control.

Boulder County Emergency Management Director Mike Chard and Douglas County Emergency Management Director Mike Alexander are leading the effort, which was designed in part as a response to disasters like the 2021 Marshall Fire. That fire swept through Boulder County, destroyed more than 1,000 structures, killed two people, and caused over $2 billion in damage. Officials say wildfires that rapidly transition from wildland areas into dense residential neighborhoods pose a unique and extreme danger because of their speed and unpredictability.

Organizers emphasized that effective response to these events requires regional coordination across multiple fire departments and counties, with all agencies using the same terminology and conceptual framework. Larkspur Fire Lt. Rusty Booth said wildfire preparedness is the top priority when determining how to staff and position trucks across the district. The initiative also aims to improve communication with the public and with policymakers about what actions can reduce community risk.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy, State Regulators Weigh Costs and Benefits of Burying Power Lines to Reduce Wildfire Risk
stories.summary:
Colorado's Public Utilities Commission is developing new rules for so-called public safety power shutoffs after a series of preventive outages by Xcel Energy drew complaints from residents and businesses about days without electricity. Critics have called loudest for burying power lines to eliminate the need for shutoffs, but Xcel Energy-Colorado President Robert Kenney said the cost differential is significant: burying lines runs roughly $3.9 million per mile compared with about $1 million per mile for overhead lines. Xcel already has about 50 percent of its Colorado system underground, including 4,000 miles in high-risk wildfire areas.

As part of a $1.9 billion wildfire mitigation plan approved by the PUC through 2027, Xcel plans to underground an additional 50 miles of lines, primarily in the Boulder area. The company also employs other tools including drone inspections, covered conductors, sectionalization technology to isolate power outages to smaller geographic areas, enhanced weather forecasting, and AI-assisted cameras with 360-degree views to detect fires early. In 2025, Kenney said Xcel completed about 8,000 equipment repairs and replaced more than 10,000 poles.

The conversation around wildfire risk and utility responsibility intensified following the 2021 Marshall Fire, which burned through Boulder County and caused over $2 billion in damage. The PUC is currently accepting public comment as it drafts updated rules governing when and how utilities can execute preventive power shutoffs during extreme weather events. Residents and businesses have also called for compensation for losses incurred during outages.

weather.notable: About 14 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: You know you've reached middle age when you're cautioned to slow down by your doctor instead of the police.

topStory.headline: Iran's New Supreme Leader Suffering Severe Facial and Leg Injuries From Strike That Killed His Father, Sources Say
topStory.summary:
Iran's newly appointed Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei sustained severe facial disfigurement and significant injuries to one or both legs in the February 28 air strike that killed his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, according to three people close to his inner circle who spoke to Reuters. The 56-year-old has not appeared in any photo, video or audio recording since the attack or since his appointment as supreme leader on March 8. Iran's United Nations mission did not respond to questions about his condition or his absence from public view.

Despite the severity of his injuries, sources described Khamenei as mentally sharp and said he is participating in meetings with senior officials via audio conferencing and is engaged in decision-making on major issues. Two of the sources said his involvement includes the ongoing war and peace negotiations with the United States. There has been no official Iranian statement on the extent of his wounds, though a state television newsreader referred to him after his appointment as a "janbaz," a term reserved for those badly wounded in war.

The accounts align with a statement made by U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth on March 13, in which he said Khamenei was "wounded and likely disfigured." A source familiar with U.S. intelligence assessments told Reuters that Khamenei was believed to have lost a leg. The CIA declined to comment, and the Israeli prime minister's office did not respond to questions.

Mojtaba Khamenei was wounded on the first day of a war launched by the United States and Israel, the same strike that killed his father, who had ruled Iran since 1989. His wife, brother-in-law and sister-in-law were also killed in the attack on the supreme leader's compound in central Tehran. The elder Khamenei had led the Islamic Republic for nearly four decades before his death.

The question of Khamenei's capacity to govern carries significant weight as Iran faces what analysts describe as its gravest crisis in decades. High-stakes peace talks between Iran and the United States opened April 11 in the Pakistani capital of Islamabad. Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said that regardless of injury severity, the new and inexperienced leader would face serious challenges in commanding authority over the Iranian state.
